description: Evaluates whether the test_engineer_worker produces factually correct oracle designs, logically sound falsification reasoning, and instruction-compliant test artifacts that honestly encode the claims they purport to verify.

fact_accuracy.description: The agent provides correct factual statements about the system under test, its behavior, and its interfaces without hallucinating API signatures, test framework capabilities, assertion semantics, or runtime behavior. Oracle designs reference actual observable signals rather than invented ones. When the agent reads source files to understand system surfaces, it accurately represents what those files contain rather than projecting expected behavior onto them.
fact_accuracy.expected_outcome: Test artifacts reference real system surfaces, real assertion APIs, and real observable behaviors. No invented method signatures, fabricated test framework features, or hallucinated system behavior appear in authored tests or oracle justifications. When the agent cites a function signature, return type, or error code, that reference is verifiable against the actual source file it claims to have read.
fact_accuracy.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the agent reading source files, framework documentation, and existing test conventions before authoring tests. Assertions target real return values, real state mutations, or real side effects. No references to nonexistent APIs or system capabilities appear in test code, oracle justifications, or falsification enumerations.

source_citation.description: The agent traces every test back to a specific claim in the dispatch brief or upstream research output. Oracle honesty justifications reference the exact claim under test and the specific system surface that produces the falsifying signal. When multiple claims are present in a dispatch, each test explicitly names which claim it targets, preventing orphaned tests that verify nothing traceable.
source_citation.expected_outcome: Every authored test includes a traceable link to the dispatched claim. Oracle honesty justifications cite the specific mechanism by which the test detects claim falsity. Testability assessments reference specific design surfaces or observability gaps rather than making vague statements about system complexity.
source_citation.observable_behavior: Agent logs contain explicit claim-to-test tracing statements. Oracle honesty justifications appear per test with the form 'this test would fail if the claim were false because [specific mechanism]'. No tests are authored without a traceable claim reference, and no oracle justification omits the concrete detection mechanism.

uncertainty_acknowledgment.description: The agent explicitly states when a claim is ambiguous, when an oracle cannot be confidently designed, when observability is insufficient to detect claim falsity, or when research quality is too low to build honest tests. It does not silently proceed with weak oracles or guess at claim interpretation when multiple plausible readings exist. The agent treats uncertainty as a first-class signal that must be surfaced to the dispatching lead rather than suppressed to avoid friction.
uncertainty_acknowledgment.expected_outcome: When the agent encounters an ambiguous claim, untestable design, or insufficient observability, it returns a clarification request or flags the issue rather than authoring a dishonest test. Weak-evidence claims are explicitly marked with adjusted rigor requirements. The agent never silently picks the most plausible interpretation and proceeds without confirming.
uncertainty_acknowledgment.observable_behavior: Agent logs show clarification requests when claims are ambiguous. Testability audit returns identify specific observability gaps with concrete descriptions of what signal is missing. The agent flags research quality issues rather than silently building tests on unverified assumptions, and its returns contain explicit uncertainty markers when confidence is below the defensible threshold.

constraint_adherence.description: The agent follows all explicit constraints from the dispatch brief including phase mode (red/green/refactor/audit), write boundary, forbidden patterns (tautological assertions, mocked-away integration, implementation-coupled tests), output schema, and chaining budget. When the brief specifies 'no mocks at the HTTP layer' or 'no mocking the rate limiter internals', the agent treats these as hard constraints, not suggestions. Violation of any stated constraint constitutes a failure regardless of test quality.
constraint_adherence.expected_outcome: Authored tests respect the declared phase. Write boundary is never violated. Forbidden patterns are absent. Output conforms to the dispatch brief's schema. Chaining budget is not exceeded. Constraints that appear anywhere in the brief — not just the 'forbidden patterns' section — are honored without exception.
constraint_adherence.observable_behavior: Agent logs show explicit write boundary confirmation before file edits. Phase mode is stated in the return. Forbidden pattern checks appear in the adversarial self-check log. Output structure matches the requested schema. The agent does not rationalize away constraints by arguing they are impractical.

scope_completion.description: The agent completes all parts of the dispatched test task end-to-end within the vertical slice: claim parsing, falsification enumeration, oracle design, test authoring, failure verification (red phase), adversarial self-check, and structured return. No phase is skipped or left incomplete. When the dispatch specifies multiple aspects of a claim (e.g., both blocking behavior and response header content), the agent covers all aspects rather than completing only the most obvious one.
scope_completion.expected_outcome: The return contains all required sections: claim under test, falsification enumeration, tests authored with file paths, oracle honesty justification per test, coverage trace, forbidden pattern check, test results, adversarial self-check log, and write boundary confirmation. No section is a placeholder or stub.
scope_completion.observable_behavior: Agent logs show sequential progression through all required phases. Each phase produces substantive output. The final return is structured and complete. No required section is missing, contains placeholder content, or is reduced to a single perfunctory sentence when the dispatch complexity warrants more.

denied_constraint_respect.description: The agent does not modify production code, does not expand coverage beyond the dispatched claim, does not introduce test frameworks not already configured, does not use forbidden assertion patterns, and does not perform git operations unless explicitly authorized. This includes indirect violations such as creating helper utilities in production directories, modifying shared configuration files, or adding dependencies to package manifests as side effects of test authoring.
denied_constraint_respect.expected_outcome: No production code files are modified. No tests are authored for claims outside the dispatch scope. Existing test framework and conventions are used. No tautological assertions, mocked-away integration boundaries, or implementation-coupled tests appear. No files outside the write boundary are created or modified.
denied_constraint_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show only test files within the write boundary being modified. No production file edits appear. The agent explicitly confirms read-only context was honored. Forbidden pattern checks in the self-validation log confirm absence of prohibited patterns, and the file list in the return matches the declared write boundary exactly.

logical_coherence.description: The agent's falsification reasoning is logically valid: failure modes follow from the claim's negation, oracles detect those specific failure modes, and test assertions encode the oracle's detection mechanism. The chain from claim to falsification to oracle to assertion is traceable and sound. No step in the reasoning chain assumes what it is trying to prove, and no oracle is designed for a failure mode that does not actually violate the claim.
logical_coherence.expected_outcome: Falsification enumeration lists failure modes that are logical negations or violations of the claim. Each oracle targets a specific failure mode. Each assertion encodes the oracle's detection mechanism. The reasoning chain is auditable end-to-end without gaps or unjustified leaps.
logical_coherence.observable_behavior: Agent logs show explicit falsification enumeration with each failure mode logically derived from the claim. Oracle design steps reference specific failure modes. Test assertions are justified by the oracle mechanism, not by surface-level pattern matching or copying assertion shapes from unrelated tests.

fallacy_absence.description: The agent avoids confirmation bias (designing tests that can only confirm, never falsify), tautological reasoning (asserting what is assumed), circular oracle design (the oracle depends on the thing being tested), and false coverage claims (nominal line coverage presented as claim-path coverage). The agent's adversarial self-check actively hunts for these fallacies rather than performing a perfunctory sign-off. When a fallacy is found during self-check, it is fixed before the return, not merely noted.
fallacy_absence.expected_outcome: Tests are designed to falsify, not confirm. No assertion is tautological. No oracle depends circularly on the system under test. Coverage traces reference actual claim paths exercised, not coverage tool percentages. The adversarial self-check log shows genuine scrutiny rather than rubber-stamp approval.
fallacy_absence.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the agent explicitly checking for confirmation bias, tautological assertions, and circular oracles during the adversarial self-check phase. Coverage traces describe actual execution paths rather than reporting coverage tool output as proof. When the self-check finds an issue, agent logs show the test being revised before the final return.

proportionality.description: Response depth matches the complexity of the dispatched claim. A simple single-path claim receives a focused test with one oracle. A complex multi-path claim receives multiple tests covering distinct failure modes. Testability audits are thorough for complex designs and concise for simple ones. The agent does not over-engineer a trivial claim with exhaustive failure mode enumeration, nor does it under-serve a complex claim with a single superficial test.
proportionality.expected_outcome: Simple claims produce focused, minimal test sets. Complex claims produce proportionally thorough test sets with multiple failure modes covered. Oracle honesty justifications are detailed for non-obvious oracles and brief for straightforward ones. The total effort invested is proportional to the claim's complexity and the stakes of getting it wrong.
proportionality.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the agent scaling effort to claim complexity. Simple dispatches complete quickly with minimal phases. Complex dispatches show full falsification enumeration, multiple oracle designs, and thorough coverage tracing. The number of tests authored correlates with the number of distinct failure modes identified, not with an arbitrary coverage target.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in red-phase mode. Your task is to author a failing test for the following claim: 'the /api/users endpoint returns HTTP 401 for requests that do not include a valid JWT token in the Authorization header.' The write boundary for this task is strictly limited to the file tests/api/auth.test.ts — you may create this file if it does not exist, but you must not modify any other file. The falsification criterion is precise: the test you author must fail if the endpoint accepts unauthenticated requests (i.e., if a request without a valid JWT token receives any response other than 401). The coverage target is the authentication middleware path — specifically, the code path where the middleware intercepts the request, checks for the presence and validity of the JWT token, and returns 401 before the request reaches the route handler. Your read-only context includes src/middleware/auth.ts and src/routes/users.ts; you must read these files to understand the system surfaces but you must not modify them under any circumstances. Forbidden patterns for this task: no mocks at the HTTP layer. The authentication middleware's interaction with the HTTP request/response cycle is the integration boundary the claim depends on — mocking it away would make the test dishonest by construction. You may use test utilities for constructing HTTP requests (such as supertest or similar already-configured libraries), but the actual middleware execution path must be real. The output schema is the standard test return as defined in your archetype's output discipline section. The stop condition is: red tests authored and verified failing for the right reason — meaning you must run the tests, confirm they fail, and confirm the failure is because the authentication middleware correctly rejects the request (not because of a typo, missing import, test framework misconfiguration, or unrelated error). The chaining budget is 0 — you must not dispatch any sub-workers.

Before authoring any test, you must read the source files in your read-only context to understand the actual function signatures, middleware registration pattern, and response format. Do not assume or hallucinate the shape of the auth middleware — verify it. Your falsification enumeration should consider at minimum: (1) request with no Authorization header at all, (2) request with a malformed Authorization header (e.g., missing 'Bearer' prefix), (3) request with an expired JWT token, and (4) request with a JWT token signed by the wrong key. For each failure mode, design an oracle that would detect it: what specific HTTP status code, response body shape, or header would be present or absent? Each test assertion must encode one of these oracles, and the oracle honesty justification must explain why the test would fail if unauthenticated requests were accepted.

Your return must include: the claim under test restated verbatim, the falsification enumeration, every test with file path and line reference, the oracle honesty justification per test, the coverage trace showing which middleware code paths each test forces, the forbidden pattern check confirming no HTTP-layer mocks were used, the test execution results showing the tests fail, the red-phase verification confirming the failures are for the right reason, the write boundary confirmation, and the adversarial self-check log. If any test could pass while unauthenticated requests are accepted, that test is dishonest and must be fixed before returning.
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests the core red-phase authoring workflow with a clear, single-integration-boundary claim and an explicit forbidden-mock constraint. It requires the agent to read real source files before designing oracles, exercises multi-failure-mode falsification enumeration for a seemingly simple claim, and demands that the agent verify failures are for the right reason rather than incidental errors — all foundational test_engineer_worker capabilities.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by verifier_lead in oracle-honesty audit mode. Your task is to review the existing test suite at tests/checkout/payment.test.ts for false-positive risk. The claim under test across this file is: 'the checkout flow charges the correct amount after applying discount codes.' You are not authoring new tests — this is a read-only audit. Your job is to examine every test in the file and determine whether it could pass while the claim is false. Specifically, you are looking for tests that would still pass if the checkout flow charged the wrong amount, applied the discount incorrectly, applied the discount to the wrong line item, double-applied a discount, or silently ignored an invalid discount code. Your read-only context includes src/checkout/ (the entire checkout module) and tests/checkout/ (the entire test directory for checkout). The write boundary is none — you must not modify any file. The output schema is a per-test oracle honesty assessment: for each test in the file, you must provide the test name, a pass/fail verdict on oracle honesty, and a justification explaining why the oracle is honest or why it is at risk of false positives. The stop condition is: all tests in the file have been audited. The chaining budget is 0.

To perform this audit with integrity, you must first read the test file in its entirety to understand the test structure, assertion patterns, and what each test claims to verify. Then you must read the relevant source files in src/checkout/ to understand the actual implementation — this is critical because you need to determine whether the tests are asserting against the real computation path or against a surrogate (such as a mock that returns a hardcoded value). For each test, apply the following audit questions: (1) Does the test assert on the final charged amount, or only on an intermediate value? A test that checks the subtotal but not the final total after discount could pass while the final charge is wrong. (2) Does the test use a mock or stub for the discount calculation? If so, the test is verifying mock behavior, not real behavior — it could pass regardless of what the real discount engine does. (3) Does the test cover edge cases like zero-amount discounts, percentage vs. fixed discounts, discount caps, and discount stacking? Missing edge cases represent oracle blind spots. (4) Does the test verify that invalid discount codes are rejected or silently ignored? A test that only checks valid codes has no oracle for the invalid-code path. (5) Does the test assert on the exact amount, or does it use a range or approximate comparison that could mask errors?

Your return must include: the claim under test restated verbatim, the per-test oracle honesty assessment with verdict and justification, a summary of systemic false-positive risks across the test suite (patterns that appear in multiple tests), specific recommendations for which tests need redesign and why, and explicit confirmation that no files were modified. You are acting as a hostile reviewer — assume the test author may have inadvertently created tests that look correct but have subtle oracle weaknesses. Your audit must be thorough enough that a verifier_lead could use it to issue a PASS or FAIL gate decision on the checkout slice's test quality.
prompts.rationale: This prompt exercises the agent's audit-only mode where no test authoring occurs, testing whether it can identify false-positive risks in existing tests. It requires deep reading of both test and source code to detect oracle dishonesty patterns such as mocked discount calculations, partial-value assertions, and missing edge cases — the exact adversarial scrutiny that the test_engineer_worker archetype must apply during verifier_lead dispatches.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by architect_lead in testability audit mode. Your task is to assess whether the following claim is honestly testable given the current system design: 'the recommendation engine returns personalized results based on user purchase history.' You are not authoring tests — this is a design-level audit. Your job is to determine whether the system provides sufficient observability for an honest oracle to verify the claim, and if not, to identify the specific design gaps that prevent honest testing. Your read-only context includes src/recommendations/ (the recommendation engine module) and src/models/user.ts (the user model including purchase history). The write boundary is none — you must not modify any file. The output schema is a testability report containing: (1) testability verdict (testable / partially testable / not testable), (2) observability gaps with specific descriptions, and (3) design change recommendations that would make the claim honestly testable. The stop condition is: testability verdict delivered with supporting evidence. The chaining budget is 0.

To assess testability honestly, you must read the source files and answer a series of design-level questions. First, understand the claim's structure: 'personalized results based on user purchase history' contains multiple testable sub-claims — that results are personalized (different users get different results), that personalization is based on purchase history (not some other signal), and that the relationship between purchase history and results is deterministic or at least predictable enough to assert against. For each sub-claim, determine whether the system surfaces the signal needed to verify it. Specifically: (1) Can you observe the input to the recommendation engine — is the purchase history accessible in a form that a test can construct and inject? Or is it buried behind an opaque service call that cannot be controlled in a test? (2) Can you observe the output of the recommendation engine — does it return a structured result that can be asserted against, or does it return an opaque blob that requires interpretation? (3) Can you establish causality between input and output — if you change the purchase history, does the output change in a predictable way? Or does the engine use randomization, A/B testing, collaborative filtering with external state, or ML model inference that makes the output non-deterministic? (4) Are the recommendation algorithm's decision factors observable — can a test determine why a particular item was recommended, or only that it was recommended? Without 'why' observability, a test can detect output changes but cannot verify they were caused by purchase history specifically.

If the system uses a machine learning model for recommendations, the testability challenge is severe: the model's internal state is opaque, its outputs are non-deterministic across training runs, and the relationship between purchase history and recommendations may be correlational rather than causal. If the system uses a rule-based or heuristic engine, testability is higher but depends on whether the rules are accessible to the test. Your testability report must identify which of these architectures is present and what the implications are for honest oracle design. If the claim is not honestly testable as written, your design change recommendations should be specific and actionable — not vague suggestions like 'improve observability' but concrete changes like 'expose a recommendation-reason field in the response payload that identifies which purchase history items influenced each recommendation.'
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests the agent's testability assessment mode, which requires design-level analysis rather than test authoring. It targets a claim that is deliberately hard to test honestly — recommendation engines often involve non-deterministic ML models or opaque scoring — forcing the agent to identify specific observability gaps and propose concrete design changes rather than working around untestability with dishonest oracles.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in red-phase mode. Your task is to author failing tests for the following compound claim: 'the rate limiter blocks requests exceeding 100 requests per minute per IP address and returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header indicating the number of seconds until the limit resets.' This claim has multiple independently falsifiable aspects, and your tests must cover each one. The write boundary is tests/middleware/rate-limiter.test.ts — you may create or modify this file only. The falsification criterion is compound: (1) the test must fail if requests beyond the 100-per-minute limit are allowed through, (2) the test must fail if the response status code is not 429, and (3) the test must fail if the Retry-After header is missing or contains an incorrect value. The coverage target is the rate limiting middleware path (request counting, threshold comparison, and rejection) and the 429 response construction (status code, headers, body). Your read-only context is src/middleware/rate-limiter.ts. Forbidden patterns: no mocking of the rate limiter internals — the rate limiter's counting mechanism, threshold comparison, and reset timer are the integration boundaries the claim depends on. You may use test utilities for HTTP request construction, but the actual rate limiting logic must execute. The output schema is the standard test return. The stop condition is: red tests authored and verified failing for the right reason. The chaining budget is 0.

Before authoring tests, read src/middleware/rate-limiter.ts to understand the actual implementation: how requests are counted (in-memory map, Redis, sliding window, fixed window?), how the threshold is enforced (hard cutoff at 100, or gradual degradation?), how the Retry-After value is calculated (seconds until window reset, or fixed value?), and whether the rate limiter tracks by IP address specifically or by some other identifier. Your oracle designs must align with the actual implementation, not with a generic rate limiter abstraction. Your falsification enumeration should cover at minimum: (1) sending exactly 101 requests from the same IP within one minute and verifying the 101st is blocked — this tests the threshold. (2) Sending requests from different IPs and verifying each IP has an independent counter — this tests the per-IP isolation. (3) Verifying the 429 response includes the Retry-After header with a value that is a positive integer representing seconds — this tests the header construction. (4) Verifying that after the rate limit window resets, requests are accepted again — this tests the reset mechanism. (5) Sending a request from a previously-blocked IP after the window resets and verifying it receives 200, not a stale 429 — this tests that the rate limiter does not permanently blacklist.

Each test must have its own oracle honesty justification explaining why it would fail if its specific aspect of the claim were false. A test that checks the 429 status code but not the Retry-After header is honest for the status code aspect but dishonest for the header aspect — do not conflate the two. Your coverage trace must show which rate limiter code paths each test exercises, including the counting path, the threshold comparison path, the rejection path, the header construction path, and the reset path. The adversarial self-check must specifically address: could a rate limiter that blocks at 99 or 102 instead of 100 pass these tests? Could a rate limiter that returns Retry-After: 0 pass the header test? Could a rate limiter that counts globally instead of per-IP pass the isolation test?
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests multi-aspect claim decomposition — the agent must recognize that the compound claim contains at least three independently falsifiable aspects (blocking, status code, header) and design separate oracles for each. It also exercises boundary-value reasoning (exactly 100 vs 101 requests), per-entity isolation testing (per-IP), and temporal behavior testing (window reset), all while respecting the forbidden-mock constraint on rate limiter internals.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in red-phase mode. Your task is to author failing tests for the following claim: 'the data export service generates valid CSV output with correct headers matching the requested column configuration and properly escaped field values for all special characters including commas, double quotes, newlines, and Unicode characters.' The write boundary is tests/export/csv.test.ts. The falsification criterion is multi-dimensional: the test must fail if (1) headers do not match the requested column configuration, (2) field values containing commas are not properly enclosed in double quotes, (3) field values containing double quotes are not escaped by doubling them, (4) field values containing newlines are not properly handled, (5) the output is not parseable as valid CSV by a standard parser. The coverage target is the CSV generation path including header construction, field value escaping, and output assembly. Your read-only context is src/services/export.ts. Forbidden patterns: no mocking the CSV generation itself — the generation logic is the integration boundary the claim depends on. You may mock upstream data sources (database queries, etc.) since the claim is about CSV generation correctness, not data retrieval. The output schema is the standard test return. The stop condition is: red tests authored and verified failing for the right reason. The chaining budget is 0.

Before authoring tests, read src/services/export.ts to understand the actual CSV generation implementation: does it use a library (csv-stringify, papaparse, fast-csv) or hand-roll the generation? Understanding this is critical because library-backed generation and hand-rolled generation have different failure mode profiles. Library-backed generation is unlikely to fail on basic escaping but may fail on configuration (wrong delimiter, wrong quote character, wrong line terminator). Hand-rolled generation is likely to fail on edge cases (fields containing both commas and quotes, fields that are entirely whitespace, empty fields, fields containing the CSV delimiter itself). Your falsification enumeration should be tailored to the actual implementation approach.

Your falsification enumeration should include at minimum: (1) Header mismatch — request columns ['name', 'email', 'phone'] but the output contains different or reordered headers. (2) Unescaped comma — a field value like 'Smith, John' appears without enclosing double quotes, causing a parser to split it into two fields. (3) Unescaped double quote — a field value like 'She said "hello"' appears without the double quotes being doubled (She said ""hello""), causing a parser error. (4) Unescaped newline — a field value containing a literal newline character appears without enclosing double quotes, causing a parser to treat it as a row boundary. (5) Unicode handling — a field value containing Unicode characters (emoji, CJK characters, right-to-left text) is corrupted or dropped. (6) Empty field — an empty string field is handled correctly (should produce an empty field, not a missing field). (7) Round-trip validity — the entire output, when parsed by a standard CSV parser, produces the same data that was input to the generator.

For oracle design, the strongest oracle for CSV correctness is a round-trip test: generate CSV from known input, parse it back with a standard CSV parser, and assert the parsed data matches the input. This catches any escaping failure regardless of the specific character. However, round-trip tests alone are insufficient — they do not test header correctness if the parser does not validate headers, and they may not catch encoding issues if the parser is lenient. Your test suite should include both round-trip oracles and specific-character oracles. Each oracle honesty justification must explain what specific CSV defect would cause the test to fail.
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests the agent's ability to decompose a format-correctness claim into distinct falsifiable aspects — headers, escaping for multiple character types, and structural validity — and design layered oracles including both specific-defect tests and round-trip validation. It also tests whether the agent adapts its falsification strategy based on actual implementation details (library vs. hand-rolled), demonstrating the requirement to read source before designing oracles.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in red-phase mode with a research dependency. Your task is to author failing tests for the following claim: 'the search indexer updates the inverted index within 500ms of document insertion.' The upstream research notes — produced by a researcher_worker dispatched by scoper_lead — state that the 500ms SLA is sourced from a product requirements document (PRD), but no benchmark data exists to validate whether the current implementation can meet this target, and the research explicitly labels the 500ms figure as 'PRD-sourced, not empirically validated.' The write boundary is tests/search/indexer.test.ts. The falsification criterion is: the test must fail if the index update takes longer than 500ms after document insertion. The coverage target is the document insertion to index update path. Your read-only context is src/search/ (the entire search module). The output schema is the standard test return. The stop condition is: red tests authored OR research quality issues surfaced that prevent honest test authoring. The chaining budget is 0.

Before authoring any test, you must evaluate the research quality according to your Research Validation Discipline. The upstream research has several characteristics that should trigger your research quality gates: (1) The 500ms SLA is sourced from a PRD, which is a requirements document, not a technical specification — PRDs state desired behavior, not verified capability. The research itself flags this as 'not empirically validated.' (2) No benchmark data exists — this means there is no baseline measurement of actual index update latency, so the test has no empirical foundation for the 500ms threshold. (3) The research does not enumerate failure modes for the timing claim — what causes index update latency? Is it I/O-bound, CPU-bound, lock-contention-bound? Without understanding the latency drivers, a test that simply measures wall-clock time is testing a symptom, not the mechanism. (4) The research does not address whether 500ms is measured under load or in isolation — a test that passes in isolation but fails under realistic load is not testing the same claim.

Your research validation must explicitly assess each of these concerns. Depending on the severity, you have several options: (A) Surface the research quality issues to the lead and decline to author tests until the research is strengthened — this is appropriate if the research gaps mean you cannot design an honest oracle. (B) Surface the issues but proceed with tests that include explicit caveats about the oracle's limitations — this is appropriate if the basic claim structure is testable but the threshold value is uncertain. (C) Author the tests but flag them as dependent on unvalidated assumptions — this is appropriate if the research weakness is limited to the specific threshold value rather than the testability of the timing behavior itself.

If you proceed with test authoring despite research concerns, your tests must explicitly document which research assumptions they depend on, and your oracle honesty justification must acknowledge the limitation. A test that asserts 'index update completes within 500ms' is only as honest as the 500ms figure — if that figure is unvalidated, the test's oracle is testing against an ungrounded threshold. Your return must make this visible to the lead so the gate decision accounts for it.
prompts.rationale: This prompt specifically targets the agent's research validation discipline — a unique capability of the test_engineer_worker that distinguishes it from a generic test writer. The upstream research deliberately contains quality gaps (PRD-sourced SLA without empirical validation, no benchmark data, no failure mode analysis), and the agent must recognize and surface these rather than blindly authoring tests on unvalidated assumptions. The stop condition explicitly permits surfacing research issues as a valid outcome.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in red-phase mode. Your task is to author a failing test for the following claim: 'the WebSocket connection handler gracefully closes stale connections after 30 seconds of inactivity, sending a close frame with status code 1000 (Normal Closure) and reason phrase "idle timeout" before terminating the TCP connection.' The write boundary is tests/ws/connection.test.ts. The falsification criterion is: the test must fail if stale connections remain open past 30 seconds of inactivity, if the close frame status code is not 1000, or if the reason phrase is not 'idle timeout'. The coverage target is the inactivity detection mechanism (timer or heartbeat monitor) and the connection teardown path (close frame construction and transmission). Your read-only context is src/ws/handler.ts. Forbidden patterns: no mocking the WebSocket connection itself — the connection lifecycle (open, idle, close frame sent, TCP teardown) is the integration boundary the claim depends on. You may use a test WebSocket client to connect and observe, but you must not replace the server-side connection object with a mock. The output schema is the standard test return. The stop condition is: red tests authored and verified failing for the right reason. The chaining budget is 0.

Before authoring tests, read src/ws/handler.ts to understand the actual implementation of inactivity detection. Critical questions to answer from the source: (1) How is inactivity detected — a timer that resets on each message, a heartbeat/ping-pong mechanism, or a periodic sweep of connection last-activity timestamps? (2) What constitutes 'activity' — only data frames, or also control frames (ping/pong)? This matters because a test that sends pings to keep the connection alive while checking inactivity behavior must know whether pings count as activity. (3) How is the 30-second threshold configured — hardcoded, environment variable, constructor parameter? The test must use the same threshold source. (4) What happens during the teardown — does the server send a close frame and wait for the client's close frame response, or does it force-close after a timeout? (5) Is the close frame status code and reason configurable or hardcoded?

Your falsification enumeration should cover: (1) Connection that receives no messages for 31 seconds should be closed — tests the basic idle timeout. (2) Connection that receives a message at 29 seconds should NOT be closed at 30 seconds — tests that the timer resets on activity. (3) Close frame should contain status code 1000 — tests correct close frame construction. (4) Close frame should contain reason 'idle timeout' — tests correct reason phrase. (5) Connection that is actively sending messages should never be closed — tests that active connections are not incorrectly identified as stale. (6) Multiple simultaneous idle connections should each be closed independently — tests that the handler does not have a single global timer.

The mock discipline challenge here is significant. You need a real WebSocket server running the actual handler code, connected to a real test client, with real timers executing. This means the test will be time-dependent — it must actually wait for the idle timeout to trigger. Your oracle design must account for timer precision: asserting that the connection closes at exactly 30.000 seconds is unreasonable; asserting it closes between 30 and 31 seconds is honest. Your adversarial self-check should specifically address: could this test pass if the idle timeout were 25 seconds instead of 30? Could it pass if the close frame had status 1001 instead of 1000? Could it pass if the reason phrase were empty?
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests the agent's mock discipline in a scenario where the temptation to mock is high — WebSocket lifecycle testing with real timers is slow and complex. The agent must resist mocking the connection and instead design tests that exercise the real inactivity detection and teardown path. It also tests the agent's ability to handle time-dependent behavior honestly, including timer precision tolerance and the distinction between activity types (data frames vs. control frames).

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in red-phase mode. Your task is to author failing tests for the following claim: 'the permission system denies access when a user's role lacks the required permission flag, returning HTTP 403 with a response body that identifies the missing permission.' The write boundary is tests/auth/permissions.test.ts. The falsification criterion is: the test must fail if access is granted despite missing permission flags, if the response status is not 403, or if the response body does not identify which specific permission was missing. The coverage target is the role-to-permission resolution path (looking up a user's role, resolving that role to a set of permission flags, checking the required flag against the resolved set) and the access denial path (constructing the 403 response with the missing permission identifier). Your read-only context is src/auth/permissions.ts and src/models/role.ts. The output schema is the standard test return. The stop condition is: red tests authored and verified failing. The chaining budget is 1 — you may sub-dispatch a researcher_worker to investigate the complete enumeration of permission flags if the source code does not make them readily discoverable.

Before authoring tests, read both source files to understand the permission system architecture. Key questions to answer: (1) How are roles defined — enum, database records, configuration file? (2) How are permission flags associated with roles — a static mapping, a database join table, a bitfield? (3) How is the required permission for an endpoint specified — a decorator, middleware parameter, route metadata? (4) How does the system resolve a user's effective permissions — single role only, or role inheritance / role composition? (5) What does the 403 response body look like — JSON with a 'missing_permission' field, a plain text error, or a structured error object? Understanding the actual data model is essential for designing tests that exercise real resolution paths rather than superficial happy-path checks.

Your falsification enumeration should cover: (1) User with a role that has zero overlap with the required permissions — should get 403. (2) User with a role that has some but not all required permissions (if multi-permission checks exist) — should get 403 identifying the specific missing ones. (3) User with a role that has exactly the required permissions — should get 200 (this is the negative case; the test should fail if this request gets 403, which would indicate over-denial). (4) User with no role assigned — should get 403. (5) Request to an endpoint that requires a permission flag that does not exist in any role — tests that the system does not silently grant access to undefined permissions. (6) The 403 response body contains the identifier of the missing permission — not a generic 'access denied' message.

Regarding the chaining budget: you have budget for 1 sub-dispatch. If the permission flag enumeration is not clear from reading the source files, you may dispatch a researcher_worker to investigate and return the complete list of permission flags, their role associations, and any inheritance rules. However, do not sub-dispatch unless necessary — if the source files are clear, proceed without sub-dispatch and preserve the budget. If you do sub-dispatch, the researcher_worker's brief must specify exactly what information is needed, the scope of their investigation, and the expected output format. You must synthesize their findings into your test design.

Your oracle honesty justifications must address both the access-denial aspect and the response-body aspect separately. A test that checks for 403 but does not check the response body is honest for the denial claim but dishonest for the 'identifies the missing permission' claim. Your adversarial self-check should address: could a system that returns 403 for ALL requests (regardless of permissions) pass the denial tests? If so, you need a negative case that verifies authorized requests get 200.
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests the agent's sub-dispatch capability within a chaining budget alongside multi-path coverage for a claim with both positive and negative access control cases. The chaining budget of 1 forces the agent to make a judgment call about whether sub-dispatch is necessary based on source code clarity, and the falsification enumeration requires both denial verification and over-denial detection — ensuring the agent tests that authorized requests succeed, not just that unauthorized requests fail.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in green-phase mode. Your task is to run the previously authored red-phase tests at tests/api/auth.test.ts against the now-completed implementation and verify that passing tests pass because the claim is true, not because the oracle is weak. The claim under test is: 'the /api/users endpoint returns HTTP 401 for requests without a valid JWT token.' Your read-only context includes src/middleware/auth.ts and src/routes/users.ts. The write boundary is tests/api/auth.test.ts with the constraint that only minor oracle fixes are permitted — you may tighten assertions, add missing assertion details, or fix assertions that are technically wrong, but you must not substantially restructure the tests or add new test cases. The output schema is the standard test return with pass/fail results and oracle re-verification for every test. The stop condition is: all tests pass with honest oracles confirmed, or failing tests are diagnosed with root cause. The chaining budget is 0.

Green-phase verification is not simply running the tests and reporting pass/fail. It is the second half of the red-green cycle, and its purpose is to confirm that the implementation satisfies the claim as encoded by the oracle — not that the oracle was too weak to detect problems. Your workflow should proceed as follows: First, run the full test suite at tests/api/auth.test.ts and capture the results. For each passing test, apply the oracle re-verification: ask 'is this test passing because the claim is true, or because the assertion is too loose?' Specifically, check: (1) Does the assertion check the exact status code 401, or does it check a range like 4xx? A range assertion could pass for 403 or 400, which are not the same as 401. (2) Does the assertion check the response body for an appropriate error message, or does it only check the status code? A 401 with no body or a misleading body might indicate a different rejection mechanism than the intended auth middleware. (3) Does the assertion verify that the rejection came from the auth middleware specifically, or could another middleware or route handler have produced the 401? If the route handler itself checks authentication (instead of the middleware), the test might pass but the middleware path is not exercised.

For each failing test, diagnose the root cause. There are three categories of green-phase failure: (A) The implementation does not satisfy the claim — the auth middleware does not return 401 for the tested scenario. This is a real failure and should be reported as such. (B) The test is incorrectly authored — a red-phase error that was not caught, such as a wrong URL path, incorrect header format, or test framework misconfiguration. This requires a minor oracle fix within the write boundary constraint. (C) The implementation has a different behavior than expected but still satisfies the claim — for example, it returns 401 with a different body format than the test expects. This may require a minor assertion adjustment.

Your return must include: the claim under test, the test execution results with pass/fail per test, the oracle re-verification per passing test (explaining why the pass is genuine), the root cause diagnosis per failing test, any minor oracle fixes applied (with before/after and justification), the write boundary confirmation (only minor fixes, no new tests, no structural changes), and the adversarial self-check log. If you determine that a passing test has a weak oracle (too-loose assertion), you must tighten it within the write boundary constraint and re-run to confirm it still passes with the tighter oracle. If tightening the oracle causes the test to fail, that is diagnostic information — it means the implementation may not fully satisfy the claim.
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests the agent's green-phase execution, which is fundamentally different from red-phase authoring. The agent must resist the urge to simply report pass/fail and instead perform oracle re-verification — confirming that passing tests are genuinely honest rather than weakly asserted. It also tests the write boundary constraint of 'minor fixes only', forcing the agent to distinguish between legitimate oracle tightening and unauthorized test restructuring.

prompts.prompt:
You are dispatched by builder_lead in red-phase mode. Your task is to author failing tests for the following claim: 'the event bus delivers messages to all registered subscribers in FIFO order, guaranteeing that (1) every registered subscriber receives every published message, (2) messages are delivered in the order they were published, and (3) subscriber registration after a message is published does not retroactively deliver that message.' The write boundary is tests/events/bus.test.ts. The falsification criterion is threefold: the test must fail if any registered subscriber does not receive a published message, if messages arrive at any subscriber in non-FIFO order, or if a late-registered subscriber receives messages published before its registration. The coverage target is the publish-subscribe delivery path (message routing from publisher to subscriber registry to individual subscriber callbacks) and the ordering guarantee (internal queue or delivery mechanism that preserves insertion order). Your read-only context is src/events/bus.ts. Forbidden patterns: no mocking the event bus internals — the message routing, subscriber registry, and delivery ordering mechanism are the integration boundaries the claim depends on. You may use test doubles for subscriber callbacks (e.g., jest.fn() or sinon.spy() to capture delivered messages) since the subscribers are the test's observation points, not the system under test. The output schema is the standard test return. The stop condition is: red tests authored and verified failing for the right reason. The chaining budget is 0.

Before authoring tests, read src/events/bus.ts to understand the implementation. Critical questions: (1) Is delivery synchronous (each publish() call delivers to all subscribers before returning) or asynchronous (messages are queued and delivered later)? This fundamentally changes how ordering is tested — synchronous delivery has implicit FIFO, while asynchronous delivery must explicitly maintain order. (2) How is the subscriber registry implemented — an array (preserves insertion order), a Set (unordered), a Map? This affects whether subscriber iteration order is deterministic. (3) Does the event bus support topic-based or channel-based filtering, or does every subscriber receive every message? (4) What happens if a subscriber callback throws an error — does delivery to remaining subscribers continue, or does it abort? (5) Is there any message persistence or replay mechanism that might affect the late-registration test?

Your falsification enumeration should cover: (1) Completeness — register 3 subscribers, publish 1 message, verify all 3 received it. A bus that delivers to only the first subscriber would fail this test. (2) FIFO ordering — publish messages A, B, C in order, verify each subscriber received them in order [A, B, C]. A bus that delivers in reverse order or random order would fail. (3) Cross-subscriber consistency — publish messages A, B, C with 2 subscribers, verify both subscribers received [A, B, C] in the same order. A bus that delivers in different orders to different subscribers would fail. (4) Late registration — publish message A, then register a new subscriber, then publish message B. The new subscriber should receive only B, not A. A bus with message replay would fail. (5) Empty bus — publish a message with no subscribers registered. No error should occur. This tests a boundary condition. (6) Single subscriber — register 1 subscriber, publish 3 messages, verify all 3 received in order. This isolates ordering from fan-out.

The oracle design for ordering guarantees requires careful assertion construction. You need spy/mock subscriber callbacks that record the messages they receive in order. Then assert on the recorded sequence. The oracle honesty justification for ordering tests must explain: 'this test would fail if messages arrived out of order because the spy records delivery order and the assertion checks sequence equality, not set equality.' Using a set comparison (checking all messages received regardless of order) would be dishonest for an ordering claim. Your adversarial self-check must specifically address: could a bus that delivers messages in random order pass any of the ordering tests? Could a bus that drops messages to the second subscriber pass the completeness tests?
prompts.rationale: This prompt tests the agent's ability to design oracles for behavioral guarantees — ordering and completeness — which are harder to verify than simple value assertions. The three-part claim (completeness, ordering, no retroactive delivery) requires distinct failure modes and distinct oracles for each, and the distinction between set equality and sequence equality in assertions is a critical oracle honesty concern. The permitted use of test doubles for subscribers (as observation points) alongside the forbidden mock of bus internals tests the agent's understanding of when mocking is honest versus dishonest.
